Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I'd cram intensely for a few days before exams rather than spreading things out evenly. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time creating mind maps and diagrams from the course material, usually late at night when my dorm was quiet. I know my attendance was terrible, but I made up for it by really diving deep into the content when I did study.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I'm involved in extracurriculars, so some weeks I barely touched course stuff, and other weeks I'd pull multiple all-nighters. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied most intensely. I'd go from zero to a hundred real quick when something was coming up.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or when I needed to check if there was something new posted that I'd missed by not attending class. Sometimes my peer support group would mention something from the LMS and I'd realize I needed to log in. I wasn't one of those students who checked it daily out of habit—it was more reactive than proactive.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me. The closest it got was during exam prep when I'd log in multiple times a day to watch videos and check discussion boards. But that was more of a burst than a routine. My peers helped normalize checking in more often through our group chats.
Q5: The lecture videos were absolutely essential for me since I missed so many classes. I'd watch them at 1.5x speed and pause to take visual notes. The discussion posts were also helpful because I could see how other students interpreted difficult concepts. Those two things together basically got me through the course.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading lists and supplementary PDFs. I had access to plenty of resources outside the LMS that worked better for my visual learning style. Some of the forum activities felt like busy work, so I'd skim them but not really engage deeply. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, it usually fell to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or if it helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual resources like diagrams, video explanations, or interactive elements grabbed my attention immediately. I also valued anything my peers recommended because their judgment usually aligned with what actually mattered for success.
Q8: Definitely different. Required activities got done no matter what—I'd push through even when I was scared about failing. Optional stuff only happened if I had extra time or was genuinely curious, which wasn't often given everything else on my plate. There's this constant calculation happening in my head about what's actually necessary versus what's nice to have.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded lecture slides that I'd annotate and turn into visual summaries. I used YouTube videos, Khan Academy, and other external sites constantly. I also had a study group through the peer support program where we'd meet in person and work through problems together—none of that showed up anywhere in the LMS.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was studying intensively but my LMS activity was basically zero. I'd download everything at once, then work from printed materials and my own notes for days. My peer study sessions were some of my most productive learning times, but they were completely invisible to the system. The LMS only saw me when I needed to grab something new.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and that single access would fuel hours of offline study. I preferred working from my own organized files rather than navigating the LMS repeatedly. It was more efficient for me to have everything in one place on my laptop where I could arrange it visually the way my brain works.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear got the better of me—I'd feel scared about falling too far behind because of my attendance, and that anxiety would actually paralyze me for a bit. When multiple deadlines from different courses piled up, or when my extracurriculars demanded more time, engaging with this specific course became really difficult. I had to consciously push through those moments.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one—when I was exhausted from extracurriculars or other coursework, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly, and sometimes this course got deprioritized even though I cared about doing well. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections until I absolutely had to face them.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. It felt like I was engaging on the surface, but nothing was actually sticking. Those sessions were basically wasted time.
Q15: I definitely postponed the assignments that scared me the most—the ones where I wasn't confident in my understanding. Even knowing they were important, the anxiety would make me push them off until the pressure of the deadline forced action. It's not logical, but fear can be paralyzing even when you know better. My resilience usually kicked in eventually, but the avoidance phase was real.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire engagement strategy. I'd have massive spikes of LMS activity in the days before something was due, then go quiet again. It wasn't healthy, but it worked for my grades. The deadline pressure actually helped me focus and overcome the fear that might otherwise keep me avoiding the material.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me. When I got positive feedback on an assignment, it boosted my confidence and made me more willing to engage with similar content. Negative or confusing feedback sometimes made me scared to attempt the next thing. The peer support really helped me process feedback constructively rather than letting it spiral into anxiety.
Q18: The course structure was flexible enough that someone like me—with terrible attendance but strong independent study skills—could still succeed. Having recorded lectures available was essential. But the structure also made it easy to disengage for long stretches, which isn't great when you're already prone to avoidance. It rewarded results over consistent participation.
Q19: The video content being available anytime made engagement much easier for my irregular schedule. What made it harder was when important information was only shared in live sessions I missed. Clear visual organization of materials helped me a lot, but some modules were cluttered and confusing to navigate, which increased my anxiety about missing something important.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking everything repeatedly without actually completing work—like they're gathering information but too scared to commit to submitting something. Or maybe they're struggling to understand and keep revisiting materials hoping it'll click. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That's literally me. I performed well because I studied effectively outside the LMS and leveraged my peer support network heavily. My exam scores and assignment quality came from deep offline engagement, not from clicking around the learning platform. The LMS was just a content delivery system for me, not where my actual learning happened.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and basic participation metrics pretty accurately. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, peer collaboration, and the emotional journey of learning. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing while doing something else. The numbers are just shadows of the real experience.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just completing tasks. It's about curiosity and investment in understanding, even when it's scary or difficult. For me, engagement often happened in conversations with peers or in my own head while staring at my visual notes—moments of real cognitive effort that transform information into knowledge.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my peer support group carried me through this course emotionally and academically. It wouldn't capture the fear I felt about my low attendance or the resilience it took to push through that anxiety. My visual study methods, my offline cramming sessions, the mind maps covering my walls—all invisible. The data shows a disengaged student, but I was actually deeply invested in mastering this material.
